question: Explain the mechanism by which the KRAS G12C mutation drives oncogenesis in non-small cell lung cancer (NSCLC) and how covalent KRAS G12C inhibitors (e.g., sotorasib, adagrasib) exploit this vulnerability at the atomic and cellular levels.
answer: The KRAS G12C mutation—characterized by substitution of glycine to cysteine at codon 12—locks Ras in its active GTP-bound state by impairing intrinsic GTP hydrolysis and rendering it insensitive to GAP-mediated inactivation. Biophysically, the G12C cysteine thiol forms a reversible covalent bond with the electrophilic acrylamide moiety of sotorasib/adagrasib, anchoring the drug within the Switch-II pocket of KRAS(G12C), which allosterically stabilizes the inactive GDP-bound conformation. Cellularly, persistent GTP loading of KRAS(G12C) drives constitutive activation of the RAF-MEK-ERK MAPK cascade and the PI3K-AKT-mTOR pathway, promoting uncontrolled proliferation, survival, and metabolic reprogramming in NSCLC cells. Clinically, sotorasib (CodeBreaK 200) improved median progression-free survival to 5.6 months versus docetaxel’s 4.5 months in pretreated KRAS G12C NSCLC, while adagrasib (KRYSTAL-12) in combination with pembrolizumab is being tested in first-line settings. Resistance arises via secondary KRAS alterations (Y96D), MAPK reactivation, or acquisition of bypass mutations in NRAS/BRAF; next-generation covalent inhibitors (GDC-6036) and tri-complex inhibitors (BI-2865, RMC-6236) targeting both active and inactive KRAS conformations are in early-phase trials. In summary, KRAS G12C oncogenesis hinges on persistent GTP loading, and covalent KRAS G12C inhibitors exploit an allosteric cysteine pocket to lock Ras in its inactive state, yielding clinically meaningful tumor regressions despite intrinsic and acquired resistance mechanisms.
